title: High-fidelity EDSR in Si/SiGe Wiggle Wells
authors: Hudaiba Soomro, Minyoung Kim, Avani Vivrekar, M. A. Eriksson, Benjamin D. Woods, Mark Friesen
abstract: Si/SiGe quantum wells that incorporate Ge concentration oscillations, known as long-period Wiggle Wells, have previously been shown to enhance the Dresselhaus spin-orbit coupling (SOC) of conduction-band electrons. Such intrinsic SOC is desirable when performing spin-qubit gate operations based on electric dipole spin resonance (EDSR) because it eliminates the need for external micromagnets. However, random-alloy disorder plays a key role in the valley physics of this materials system by spatially randomizing the valley phase $φ_{s,s}$, and has not been fully accounted for in recent EDSR analyses. Here, we show that alloy disorder affects EDSR in two main ways. First, the Rabi frequency $Ω$ acquires a dependence on the valley phase, given by $\cosφ_{s,s}$, which causes spatial randomization of $Ω$. Despite this variability, we show that fast EDSR can be achieved at most locations across a given sample. Second, a new Rabi driving mechanism emerges, enabled by valley dipoles that result from disorder and the hybridization of ground and excited valley states in response to an EDSR driving field. This mechanism is dominant in regions of low valley splitting. Alloy disorder can therefore strengthen EDSR, but it can also cause gradients in $Ω$ that lead to dephasing in the rotating frame. We explore this problem by first identifying "sweet spots," where EDSR is relatively insensitive to electric-field fluctuations. We then show that high-fidelity Rabi oscillations can be achieved in the presence of realistic charge noise. Our results demonstrate that the Wiggle Well is a promising platform for high-quality, micromagnet-free gate operations.
summary: This paper explores how the intrinsic spin-orbit coupling in Si/SiGe Wiggle Wells can be used for high-fidelity spin qubit control without external micromagnets. It demonstrates that despite the presence of random-alloy disorder, specific 'sweet spots' exist where gates are highly resistant to charge noise. The work provides a roadmap for achieving high-quality, micromagnet-free EDSR in silicon-based quantum computing.
main_problem: Investigating how random-alloy Ge disorder and electric-field fluctuations affect the stability and fidelity of Electric Dipole Spin Resonance (EDSR) in Si/SiGe Wiggle Wells.
main_result: The study identifies that while alloy disorder causes spatial randomization of the Rabi frequency, a new disorder-enabled valley-dipole mechanism can enhance EDSR, and high-fidelity gate operations are achievable at identified 'sweet spots' that are robust against charge noise.
method: The authors use Schrieffer-Wolff transformations to derive an effective 2D spin Hamiltonian, Floquet theory for numerical verification, and statistical modeling (Cholesky decomposition) to simulate spatial maps of alloy disorder and noise impacts.
model_or_system: The system consists of electron spin qubits in Si/SiGe 'Wiggle Well' quantum dots, featuring Ge concentration oscillations designed to enhance Dresselhaus spin-orbit coupling.
key_observables: Rabi frequency (orbital and valley contributions), valley splitting, dephasing rate (Gamma_Rabi), and the Rabi quality factor (Q_Rabi).
important_parameters: Ge oscillation wavelength (~1.6 nm), valley splitting (Ev), in-plane and vertical electric field fluctuations, and the ratio of valley-orbit coupling to valley splitting.
main_assumptions: The energy hierarchy is assumed to be orbital splitting >> valley splitting >> Zeeman energy; vertical-lateral coupling is assumed to be weak; and the driving field is in the high-frequency limit.
figures_summary: Figures include probability distributions of Rabi frequency components, spatial maps of valley splitting and phases, and comparisons between analytical predictions and numerical Floquet theory results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Wiggle Well platform, derives an effective EDSR theory via Schrieffer-Wolff transformation, analyzes the impact of alloy disorder on Rabi frequency and dephasing, investigates the robustness of 'sweet spots' against in-plane and vertical charge noise, and concludes with a feasibility assessment for micromagnet-free gates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of EDSR operation in a long-period Si Wiggle Well. (a) A quantum well is formed between SiGe barriers with Ge concentration oscillations. A single-electron qubit is shown inside the well, under plunger gate P1, and confined between the barrier gates B1 and B2. (Dimensions not shown to scale.) An ac electric field δF(t) is experienced in the plane of the qubit, due to an ac voltage Vac applied to the top gates. We also assume a vertical electric field F = Fzˆz and a static magnetic field B = Bˆx. Wiggle Well concentration oscillations of wavelength λ = 1.57 nm cause a strong enhancement of the spin-orbit coupling, which enables fast EDSR. (b) For conventional...

FIG. 2. Rabi frequency distributions in the presence of alloy disorder. (a) Probability distributions of Ωv, Ωorb, and the total Rabi frequency Ω= Ωv +Ωorb, all scaled by the conven- tional EDSR frequency Ω0, defined in Eq. (14). (b) Probabil- ity distribution of the matrix-element ratio |∆py,s|2/|∆s,s|2, which contributes to Ωv as shown in Eq. (15). Here, the dashed purple line corresponds to the median value of 0.5. (c) Probability distributions of the cosine of the valley phase ϕs,s, and valley-phase modulation factor, F(ϕs,s, ϕpy,s), de- fined in the main text. (d) 2D color-scale histogram of F and cos ϕs,s, showing positive correlations between the two distri- butions.
FIG. 3. Typical disorder-induced landscapes of parameters that play an important role in EDSR. (The same random-alloy realization is used in all panels, across the same 100×100 nm2

FIG. 4. Identifying high-fidelity regions of EDSR. (a) Quality- factor map, QRabi(x, y), based on the same disorder profile used in Fig. 3, showing two extreme types of behavior: high- fidelity behavior (dark red, QRabi ≥104), coinciding with the sweet spots indicated in Fig. 3(f), and low-fidelity behavior (dark blue, QRabi ≤10), associated with valley vortices and regions of high dephasing or low Rabi frequencies. (b) His- tograms of quality-factor values: red describes contributions from Ωorb only, while blue describes the total QRabi. FIG. 5. Spatial maps of scaled dephasing rates and valley phases (ϕs,s and ϕpy,s), for the same alloy-disorder realization as previous figures. (a) The scaled Rabi dephasing rate, including electric field fluctuations in any direction. (b), (c) Dephasing- rate maps arising from in-plane and vertical field fluctuations, respectively.
